coredata.dc:title: A facile approach to disperse metakaolin for promoting compressive strength of cement composites
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Liu, Jian-Wei
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Metakaolin (MK), a highly reactive supplementary cementitious material, is extensively employed in cement composites to improve their compressive strength. Nevertheless, MK suffers from extremely poor dispersion, resulting in agglomerating in the cement-based materials, thus failing to exhibit its positive effects fully. To overcome this drawback, for the first time, this work elaborately developed a facile graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic dispersion approach to improve the dispersity of MK effectively. Furthermore, it investigated effect of as-obtained MK (GMK) on compressive strength of cement composites. More specifically, GMK owned a smaller size and a larger specific surface area and presented a higher dispersity than UMK (namely, MK was identically treated by ultrasonic dispersion only without GQDs) and original MK. And in light of the characteristics of three types of MKs, the dispersing mechanism of MK caused by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was rationally ascribed to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs. Crucially, GMK purely by the incorporation of 1.0 wt.% (by weight of cement) demonstrated a significant enhancement of 31.03% for the 28-day compressive strength of mortar; While UMK had little influence on the compressive strength of mortar under the identical conditions. And based on the comprehensive analyses of hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of different cement pastes, the most significant positive effect of highly dispersed GMK purely by a dosage of 1.0 wt.% on the compressive strength of cement composites was accredited to the fact that GMK was able to accelerate hydration process, boost the formation of C−(A)−S−H and refine the microstructure. This study firstly provided a facile, efficient and low-cost method to fabricate high-dispersity GMK, and verified its remarkable promotion effect on compressive strength of cement composites. These observations would improve the benefits and reduce the cost of MK-like materials applied to cement-based materials.

head.ce:title: A facile approach to disperse metakaolin for promoting compressive strength of cement composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Durability for Marine Civil Engineering, Key Lab of Coastal Urban Resilient Infrastructure, MOE, College of Civil and Transportation Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, Guangdong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metakaolin (MK), a highly reactive supplementary cementitious material, is extensively employed in cement composites to improve their compressive strength. Nevertheless, MK suffers from extremely poor dispersion, resulting in agglomerating in the cement-based materials, thus failing to exhibit its positive effects fully. To overcome this drawback, for the first time, this work elaborately developed a facile graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic dispersion approach to improve the dispersity of MK effectively. Furthermore, it investigated effect of as-obtained MK (GMK) on compressive strength of cement composites. More specifically, GMK owned a smaller size and a larger specific surface area and presented a higher dispersity than UMK (namely, MK was identically treated by ultrasonic dispersion only without GQDs) and original MK. And in light of the characteristics of three types of MKs, the dispersing mechanism of MK caused by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was rationally ascribed to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs. Crucially, GMK purely by the incorporation of 1.0 wt.% (by weight of cement) demonstrated a significant enhancement of 31.03% for the 28-day compressive strength of mortar; While UMK had little influence on the compressive strength of mortar under the identical conditions. And based on the comprehensive analyses of hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of different cement pastes, the most significant positive effect of highly dispersed GMK purely by a dosage of 1.0 wt.% on the compressive strength of cement composites was accredited to the fact that GMK was able to accelerate hydration process, boost the formation of C−(A)−S−H and refine the microstructure. This study firstly provided a facile, efficient and low-cost method to fabricate high-dispersity GMK, and verified its remarkable promotion effect on compressive strength of cement composites. These observations would improve the benefits and reduce the cost of MK-like materials applied to cement-based materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cement composites as the most extensively used building materials play an indispensable role in the development of economy and society [1], but still suffer from low mechanical strength when employed in large-scale and high-rise engineering structures [2]. To address this issue, a wide range of nanomaterials and supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) have acted as additives to improve the mechanical properties of cement-based materials [3–11]. With reference to nanomaterials such as SiO2 [3], graphene oxide [4], TiO2 [5] and Al2O3 [6], SCMs primarily including furnace slag [7], fly ash [8], silica fume [9], calcined clay [10] and metakaolin (MK) [11] demonstrate a more promising potential owing to their low cost and simple preparation process. Among them, MK as an 2D aluminosilicate material is fabricated by calcining kaolin, and has attracted increasing interest, benefiting from its wide distribution of raw materials, simple preparation process and high pozzolanic reactivity. For example, Zhang et al. reported that by optimizing the pore structure and improving the microscale compactness, the addition of 5 wt.% nano MK could achieve 15.13% improvement for the 3-day compressive strength of cement paste [12]. Apart from enhancing early mechanical properties of cement pastes, Qian et al. adopted 15 wt.% highly dispersed MK to replace cement, accomplishing nearly doubled enhancement for the 28-day compressive strength of mortar [13]. In addition, the incorporation of MK exhibited an effective role in suppressing alkali-silica reaction-induced volume expansion [14], and improving the durability of cement composites [15].

The employment of MK in cement-based materials has made tremendous progress, but the related research is still far from sufficiency. There are a few thorny problems yet to be overcome, of which poor dispersity of MK is the dominant challenge. As is well known, small-sized materials including MK are immensely easy to agglomerate in aqueous solution by virtue of their high specific surface area, interface energy and interparticle forces [13], and their agglomeration inevitably becomes severe in cement matrix with strong alkali and high-metal ion content [2]. The serious agglomeration of small-sized materials gives rise to stress concentration and deteriorative rheological behavior of cement composites, enormously reducing its positive effectiveness [2,16]. Meanwhile, economic cost is sharply increased due to compensatory addition of small-sized materials [17]. To maximize the benefits of small-sized materials for cement matrix, multitudes of methods such as ultrasonic treatment [12,18,19], high-speed blending [16], chemical modification [17,20], addition of surfactants [21,22] and in-situ growth [1,23–25] have been developed to promote their dispersity. However, few above-mentioned approaches are considered to be facile, low-cost, efficient, scalable and eco-friendly on account of their inescapable demerits. In detail, ultrasonic treatment is the most commonly employed approach to disperse small-sized materials toward improving the mechanical properties [26]. Unfortunately, this approach cannot prevent reaggregation of small-sized materials. Ma’s group developed high-speed blending to disperse cellulose nanofibers, and revealed that the treated cellulose nanofibers enhanced the compressive and flexural strength of cement. Whereas, the enhancement was mediocre, only reaching around 20% [16], suggesting that the high-speed blending failed to achieve high dispersion efficiency. Chemical functionalization aims to attach functional groups or molecules to the surface of small-sized materials, thereby overcoming their aggregation. Lv’s group [20] and Feng’s group [17] grafted polycarboxylate superplasticizer onto graphene oxide and SiO2 to produce well dispersed composites toward regulating the cement hydration products and accelerating cement hydration respectively. Nevertheless, this grafting process was complicated and time-consuming. Besides, Wang’s group [21,22] investigated the effect of different surfactants on the dispersion of SiO2, and uncovered that surfactant-modified SiO2 could accelerate hydration and improve strength of cement pastes. But surfactants are principally derived from petroleum-based chemical materials, and their preparation consumes a lot of energy and emits massive carbon emissions [27,28]. Meanwhile, some surfactants tend to adversely affect the performance of cement. More recently, Ding [23], Yao [24], Liu [1] et al. adopted the in-situ growth method to grow carbon nanotubes, graphene, SiO2 on cement/iron tailings particles respectively, alleviating the dispersion issue and enhancing the effectiveness of small-sized materials. However, this method was subject to complex processes, hard to fulfill scalable manufacturing. More importantly, it did not apply to MK dispersion since MK could not be in-situ synthesized. To address this issue, Qian et al. used tannic acid as the admixture to successfully disperse MK by magnet stirring, and demonstrated that the 28-day compressive strength of mortar incorporating 15 wt.% dispersed MK was drastically improved by 97%; while the addition of MK decreased to 10 wt.%, the compressive strength was greatly compromised to 55%, deducing that the MK dispersion induced by tannic acid potentially owned a low efficiency as the compressive strength of mortar was only significantly boosted by a mass of MK incorporation. But massive MK adding clearly increased the cost and lowered the workability of cement-based materials. Therefore, developing a simple, cost-effective, scalable and environmentally friendly route to efficiently disperse MK, and then accomplishing the compressive strength of cement composites by a small quantity of MK addition is highly desirable.

Fortunately, a facile graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation approach was deliberately developed to efficaciously produce high-dispersity single/few-layered 2D materials (graphene oxide, MoS2, h-BN, WS2 and C3N4) from bulk materials [2,29–32], and even achieved the optimal exfoliation yield of 100% [30]. GQDs were regarded to simultaneously serve as green exfoliation and stabilizer agents to exfoliate and stabilize 2D materials, thus affording a better dispersity for 2D materials than that of GQDs-free ultrasonic exfoliation [30]. This method above provided a novel, low-cost, simple, scalable and eco-friendly route to disperse 2D materials. And more importantly, the as-obtained highly dispersed C3N4 accelerated hydration and refined microstructure of cement pastes, thereby dramatically enhancing their compressive strength [2]. Furthermore, it must be pointed out that GQDs as a new carbon-based nanomaterial are composed of the carbon core and oxygen/nitrogen-containing surface groups [33]. Generally, GQDs with fluorescence as instinct performance generally possess small size within 10 nm, and their carbon cores own single/several layers of graphene crystalline structures [34]. On account of their low-cost and facile synthesis, excellent water solubility, low toxicity and environmental friendliness, GQDs have been extensively applied to biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, sensing and lubrication [35–38]. However, up to now, no reports have investigated the dispersion effect of GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation on 2D MK, not to mention the influence of the treated MK on compressive strength of cement composites.

In light of this, the present work firstly designed a facile GQDs-assisted ultrasonic strategy to effectively disperse MK, and comprehensively studied the influence of as-obtained MK (GMK) with a small dosage of 1.0 wt.% on compressive strength of cement composites. More specifically, TEM, sedimentation method and BET were performed to compare the dispersion behaviors of GMK, UMK (namely, MK was identically treated by ultrasonic dispersion without GQDs) and original MK, testifying favorable dispersion effect of GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment on MK. And based on characteristics of three types of MKs, the dispersing mechanism of MK was rationally proposed. Crucially, the influence of different MKs with a small incorporation of 1.0 wt.% on compressive strength of mortars was studied. And according to the comprehensive analyses of hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of MK-modified cement pastes, the reason for the positive effect of GMK on compressive strength of cement composites was revealed. These findings would conduce to surmounting the barrier of poor dispersity of MK-like materials, thereby efficiently applying MK-like materials with a small dosage to enhance the performance of cement composites.

Ordinary Portland cement (OPC) with the type of PI 52.5R according with the Chinese Standard GB175 and GB176 was employed to prepare cement-based specimens, and its physical properties and chemical compositions were listed in Table 1 and Table 2 , respectively. Concentrated nitric acid and naphthalene were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. MK with an average diameter size of 3.75 μm was purchased from Hengyuan New Materials Co., Ltd. (Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province, China). Deionized (DI) water of 18 MΩ cm−1 was used throughout.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of GQDs, UMK, and GMK
ce:section.ce:para: The one-pot hydrothermal method based on the literature [39] with a few modifications was adopted to synthesize GQDs. In detail, naphthalene of 2.0 g was added to concentrated nitric acid (80 mL), and then was heated at 80 °C for 12 h under a constant magnetic stirring. After that, the obtained mixture was filtered and dried to obtain ca. 3.0 g nitrated naphthalene powder. Subsequently, 1.2 g nitrated naphthalene was added to 100 mL 0.1 M NaOH solution, and then the mixed solution was sealed in a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture went through neutralization, high-speed centrifugation, filtration (through a 0.2 μm filter membrane) and dialysis to remove insoluble carbon-based products, sodium salts and unreacted small molecules of the raw material. Finally, via rotary evaporation concentration and vacuum freeze-drying, the powdered GQDs was obtained.

GMK i.e., high-dispersity MK was fabricated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation. The specific steps were as follows. 10 g original MK was added to 500 mL aqueous solution with 0.4 mg/mL GQDs (Namely, the mass ratio of original MK and GQDs was 50:1), and then underwent ultrasonic treatment (720 W, 20 kHz) in a water bath at 10 °C for 1 h. After that, a part of the mixed solution was freeze-dried to the solid-state light brown GMK for characterization analyses. The remainder mixture was used to prepare cement-based samples. UMK shared the same preparation processes with GMK, only without incorporating GQDs. Fig. 1 illustrated the preparation schematic of UMK and GMK.

To investigate the effect of UMK and GMK with small incorporation on the compressive strength of mortars, 1.0 wt.% (by weight of cement) UMK and GMK were acted as the additive to prepare mortar specimens under the same conditions, and named as UMK group and GMK group, respectively. The preparation processes were as follows. Firstly, 250 mL UMK aqueous solution containing 5 g UMK was directly obtained by ultrasonic treatment. Secondly, the UMK aqueous solution was incorporated into the cement and sand hybrid with the water-cement ratio (w/c) of 0.5 after the cement and sand hybrid was mechanically mixed at the low speed for 30 s in a container, and then all the mixture underwent low-speed blending for 30 s, high-speed blending for 30 s, suspension for 90 s and high-speed blending for 60 s. It must be stressed that no other admixtures were added. Thirdly, after mechanical stirring, the obtained mortar was cast into molds with the dimension of 40 mm × 40 mm × 40 mm. Finally, these mortar samples were placed in a curing chamber with the temperature of 20 ± 2 °C and a relative humidity of > 95%, and then demolded after 24 h and cured until the compression testing at corresponding age. GMK group or blank group (labeled as C) shared similar preparation processes except that UMK was replaced by GMK or UMK was removed respectively. The mix proportions were shown in Table 3 . In addition, the cement paste specimens of various groups for phase composition and microstructure characteristics were prepared through similar preparation processes only without sand incorporation, and the dimension of mold was 15 mm × 15 mm × 15 mm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Brucker MTP-3D infinity, Germany) were used to characterize the morphology of the as-produced GQDs. TEM (HITACH, Japan) with the type of HT7700 was employed to confirm the size of different MKs. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brucker D8 Advance, Germany) patterns, Raman (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA) spectra and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) spectra were adopted to reveal the structure information of GQDs. And XRD patterns of three classes of MKs were obtained by the same X-ray diffractometer (Brucker D8 Advance, Germany). Nitrogen adsorption analyses included the measurement of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and pore size distribution. BET specific surface area of three types of MKs was disclosed using a NOVA4200E micropore instrument (Quantachrome, USA), and the corresponding pore size distribution was unveiled by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory.

Three types of MK aqueous solutions with the same concentration (20 mg/mL) were poured into a 10 mL graduated cylinder respectively. After violent shaking, all the solutions were stood for 3 d to observe their sedimentation. The height of precipitation was used to determine the dispersity of the three kinds of MKs in the aqueous solution.

The fluidity of fresh mortars was measured by the flow table test based on China’s National Standard GB/T 2419-2005. A flow mold (an upper inner diameter of 70 ± 0.5 mm, a bottom inner diameter of 100 ± 0.5 mm and a height of 60 ± 0.5 mm) was employed. The mold filled with fresh mortars was pulled up vertically. Subsequently, the flow table began dropping 25 times at a consistent rate immediately. When the flow table stopped, the mortars’ maximum diffusion diameter and vertical direction diameter were measured, and the average value was recorded as the final result.

Rheological parameters of fresh mortars were performed using the RM 100 PLUS rheometer (Lamy Rheology Instruments Company, France) at 22 ± 3 °C, and the measuring system was MS-R3. The fresh mortars were placed in a standard measuring cup with a diameter of 42 mm and a height of 100 mm, and the measuring rotor of ST30-4 V-40 cylinder spindle with a diameter of 30 mm and a length of 40 mm was employed. To ensure the uniformity of the measurement results, the fresh mortars were pre-sheared at a shear rate of 100 s−1 for 60 s. Then, the mortar was continuously stirred and the shear rate was gradually reduced to 0 s−1 for 60 s. Finally, the yield stress and plastic viscosity of the fresh mortars were calculated on a basis of the Bingham model according to the relationship between the shear stress and shear rate obtained from the rheological test, as shown in Equation. (1).
(1)
τ =
τ 0
+ μ γ
Where τ was shear strength (Pa); τ0 was yield stress (Pa); μ was plastic viscosity coefficient (Pa · s); γ was shear rate (s−1).

According to Chinese national standard GB/T 17671-1999, the compressive strength of different mortars groups was performed after 1-, 3-, 7-, and 28-day curing age. The hydration heat evolution containing cumulative heat release and heat flow evolution were recorded by isothermal calorimeter at 23 ± 2 °C for 70 h based on ASTMC1702. The samples were prepared as follows: 7.5 g paste material of each group (the mix proportions were agreed with Table 3 only without sand) was stirred for roughly 10 s, and then immediately placed in an airtight container for the hydration heat evolution testing. XRD and thermogravimetric analysis/differential thermogravimetric (TGA/DTG) were adopted to explore the phase composition and hydration evolution. The samples were obtained as follows: The 28-day curing cement paste specimens were immersed in anhydrous ethanol for 3 d to terminate hydration, and then vacuum-dried at 50 °C until their mass were unchanged, finally ground into uniform powder with the size of less than 75 μm. For TGA/DTG testing, roughly 50 mg as-obtained sample powder was taken for each measurement, and TGA/DTG curves were recorded from 30 to 1000 °C in a flowing N2 atmosphere using a STA-409PC Thermogravimetric Analyzer (USA). The microstructure and morphology of hydration products were detected on a MIRA LMS scanning electron microscope (TESCAN, Czech). To improve the quality of SEM images, all the samples were sprayed with gold before testing. The porosity and pore size distribution were further determined by BJH theory and mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP, AutoPore IV 9500, Micromeritics, USA).

TEM and AFM images (Fig. 2 ) were used to characterize the size distribution and topographic height of GQDs. TEM images (Fig. 2a) showed that GQDs were spherical and monodispersed particles, and had no aggregation phenomenon. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM; inset in Fig. 2a) displayed that the lattice spacing of GQDS was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) plane. The sizes of GQDs were ca. 2.81 nm on average and 1.60–4.64 nm in range, obtained by randomly counting more than 100 particles (Fig. 2b). AFM images (Fig. 2c) further testified that GQDs owned excellent dispersion, consist with the finding of TEM. And the topographic heights of the as-obtained GQDs were between 0.7 and 1.7 nm (Fig. 2d), indicating that double/few-layer GQDs were prepared.

XRD patterns, Raman and FTIR spectra were further used to confirm the structure of the as-synthesized GQDs (Fig. 3 ). There was only a distinct broad peak at 27.2° in the XRD patterns (Fig. 3a), uncovering the presence of disordered carbon [40]. Namely, GQDs possessed the graphitic crystallinity structure. As illustrated in Raman spectra (Fig. 3b), two typical peaks at around 1370 and 1574 cm−1 were assigned to the D band (disordered sp3 defects) and G band (sp2 carbon) respectively, and the intensity ratio of two bands (I D/I G) is 0.87, further suggesting that the GQDs owned a high degree of crystallinity. FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b) were employed to analyze the surface groups of as-prepared GQDs. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the absorption bands at 3444, 1388 and 1070 cm−1 verified the presence of −OH, C−OH and C−O−C groups [30,39,41]. These abundant oxygen-containing groups endowed GQDs with excellent water solubility, favorable to their applications in cement related fields. Besides, an obvious peak at 1592 cm−1 was attributed to the C=C/C=O groups [42,43]. The C=C group testified the sp2 carbon structure of the GQDs, in agreement with the finding from TEM images, XRD patterns and Raman spectra. Based on above analyses, it could be seen that the as-prepared GQDs as well as the carbon with abundant oxygen-containing groups and high crystallinity sp2 structure, these results shown that the prepared GQDs with a typical amphiphilic structure had the hydrophobic single/few-layer graphite structure in core and hydrophilic oxygenous groups on the surface, thereby conferring outstanding exfoliation behaviors on GQDs [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of three types of MKs

ce:section.ce:para: Typical TEM images (Fig. 4 ) were used to directly observe the morphologies of three kinds of MKs. A great amount of original MK showed a serious agglomeration state owing to its large surface energy, leading to the fact that its actual size (mainly distributed between 6 and 10 μm) (Fig. 4a) was larger than its nominal size (3.75 μm). Proverbially, the agglomeration of MK tends to play a severely negative effect on the properties of cement composites. Regarding UMK obtained by ultrasonically dispersing MK, its agglomerated degree was slightly reduced, since it illustrated a thinner thickness but a similar size with reference to MK (Fig. 4b), deducing that ultrasonic processing possibly accomplished exfoliation of MK, thereby endowing UMK thinner thickness; However, it failed to prevent reaggregation of MK, resulting in its unchanged size. As a result, ultrasonic treatment was ineffective in dispersing MK. In sharp contrast to MK and UMK, GMK prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment underwent remarkably change, with its thickness and size (less than 3 μm) dramatically reduced (Fig. 4c). Thus, among three classes of MKs, GMK possessed the lowest agglomerated degree and the best dispersion, advantageous for its employment in cement-based materials. All of the observations drawn from TEM images implied that pure ultrasonic processing had little positive effect on MK dispersity; Whereas GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation could effectively disperse MK.

To further verify the influence of two ultrasonic treatments on the dispersity of MKs, the sedimentation experiment was conducted (Fig. 5 ). After violent shaking, three types of MK solutions showed similar dispersed state without distinct difference; But their solution colors were a little different: MK and UMK solutions held the milk white color, while on account of the combination of MK and GQDs, GMK solution displayed white gray color. After three-day standing, three classes of MK solutions unveiled obviously diverse states: MK solution became colorless and transparent, and the height of precipitation was 10 mm; And UMK solution with sediment height of around 8 mm only revealed light white color in the lower part of the measuring cylinder; As for GMK solution, its precipitation height was only 5 mm, and plentiful dispersing UMK made the solution turbid. As is well-known, the less precipitation there is, the better the particle dispersion; And remaining section of particles is considered to be dispersed in the solution. Thus, GMK presented the best dispersion, follow by UMK, further substantiating that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment endowed MK with better dispersity.

The specific surface area (SBET) and pore structure of the three kinds of MKs were confirmed by nitrogen adsorption method. Their N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and the BJH pore size distributions were shown in Fig. 6 and Table 4 . All the adsorption–desorption curves were in line with type-IV adsorption characteristics and possessed H3-type hysteresis loops, implying the presence of a large number of mesopores in the layered MKs (Fig. 6a). In comparison with that of MK and UMK, the pore volume within 50 nm for GMK was much higher (Fig. 6b), indicating that GMK owned more pores within 50 nm. According to calculation, GMK had the largest SBET of 6.134 m2g−1, increasing by 24.6% and 10.3% with reference to MK (4.924 m2g−1) and UMK (5.559 m2g−1), respectively. Simultaneously, total pore volume (Vtotal) of GMK was higher than that of MK and UMK. These findings evidenced that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic processing presented a better dispersion effect on MK than pure ultrasonic treatment, occasioning the fact that GMK possessed larger values of SBET and Vtotal.

Fig. 7 depicted the crystalline structure of three types of MKs using XRD. All the MKs shared the identical characteristic peaks, proving that ultrasonic treatment with/without GQDs failed to change the crystal structure of MK, and the treated MKs still maintained the same chemical structure to the original MK. Hence, the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic processing could effectively delaminate MK and reduce its size to enhance dispersity of GMK.

To sum up, the observations from TEM, sedimentation and nitrogen adsorption testing as well as XRD disclosed that GMK possessed smaller size and larger SBET than MK and UMK, thereby demonstrating a better dispersity. And GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could efficiently peel off MK and decrease its size, but did not damage crystal structure of MK. Therefore, GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation provided a novel and simple avenue to disperse MK, and the as-obtained highly dispersed GMK was expected to efficaciously improve the properties of cement composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exfoliation and dispersion mechanism of MK
ce:section.ce:para: On basis of characteristics above, the exfoliation and dispersion mechanism of MK induced by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was rationally assigned to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs (Fig. 8 ). More specifically, MK with a multilayered structure tended to agglomerate in aqueous solution due to its high specific surface area, interface energy and interparticle forces, proved by TEM; While GQDs with double/few-layer graphite structure and abundant oxygen-containing groups were monodispersed and shared a ultrasmall average size of ca. 2.81 nm, revealed by TEM, AFM and FTIR. Proverbially, ultrasonic treatment can produce a strong tensile stress, weakening the interlaminar van der Waals force of 2D materials, enlarging their interlamellar spacing and allowing them to exfoliate [2]. However, once the ultrasound is removed, 2D materials tend to reaggregate. Hence, pure ultrasonic treatment could not affectively peel off and disperse MK, leading to an unobtrusive enhancement in dispersity for UMK. After incorporating GQDs, GQDs with outstanding water solubility well dispersed in solution, and functioned as the intercalation agent to intercalate into layers of MK due to their ultrasmall size. Subsequently, GQDs contributed to strengthen the tensile stress provided by ultrasonic processing, and concurrently GQDs could generate a new strong electrostatic attraction force of H-bonds between their oxygen-containing groups and water molecules. Thus, GQDs also served as the exfoliation agent to more efficiently delaminate the layer from MK and reduce its size, resulting in a better dispersity of GMK. Besides, GQDs could also couple on the surface of GMK, acting as the surfactant agent to resist its re-aggregation, unveiled by TEM, sedimentation and nitrogen adsorption testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluidity and rheology of fresh mortars
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of GQDs, UMK and GMK on fluidity and rheological parameters of fresh mortars were determined. As shown in Fig. 9 a, compared with C group (260 mm), G group possessed a slightly larger spread diameter (265 mm), while UMK and GMK samples held smaller spread diameters (250 and 253 mm, respectively). Thus, GQDs with a small dosage of 0.02 wt.% played a positive role in the fluidity, possibly since numerous –COOH groups endowed GQDs with the water-reducing action. Whereas, UMK and GMK demonstrated negative influence on the fluidity of fresh mortars, attributed to the increasing water requirement induced by MK particles, and their irregular sheet structure [44]. In addition, although GMK had the better dispersity than UMK, GMK group illustrated the marginally better fluidity than UMK group, which could be assigned to the coupling of GQDs on the surface of GMK. This finding above was further supported by the rheological parameters. The rheological curves and parameters based on the Bingham model were shown in Fig. 9b and Table 5 , respectively. Compared with those of C group, the yield stress and plastic viscosity values of G group were slightly lower (from 17.1414 to 15.6818 Pa, and from 0.2106 to 0.2004 Pa∙s, respectively); On the contrary, the UMK and GMK groups had higher yield stress values (19.9310 and 18.2677 Pa, respectively) and plastic viscosity values (0.2329 and 0.2187 Pa∙s, respectively). In conclusion, a small addition of GQDs (0.02 wt.%) slightly promoted the fluidity and rheology of fresh mortar; While a small dosage of UMK (1.0 wt.%) and GMK (1.0 wt.%) played a marginally negative effect on the fluidity and rheology of fresh mortars.

To testify the effect of UMK and GMK on mechanical properties of cement composites, the compressive strength of mortar specimens with/without 1.0 wt.% MK after at 1-, 3-, 7- and 28-day curing was investigated (Fig. 10 ). With respect to that of blank group without MK (i.e., C group), the compressive strength of mortar with 1.0 wt.% UMK was basically unchanged at any curing age. This was potentially ascribed to the fact that the small amount of poorly dispersed UMK failed to exert pozzolanic, nucleation and filling effects on cement. On the sharp contrary, the compressive strength of GMK-modified mortar was significantly improved regardless of the curing age. To be specific, at 1-, 3-, 7- and 28-day curing, the compressive strengths of GMK group were 11.0 MPa, 29.8 MPa, 39.8 MPa and 60.7 MPa, increased by 46.67%, 19.49%, 14.05% and 31.03% than those of blank group (7.5 MPa, 25.0 MPa, 34.9 MPa and 46.3 MPa), respectively. This observation was consistent with the previous report [12]. Namely, a small addition of small-sized MK could distinctly enhance the compressive strength of cement composites. The different effects of UMK and GMK on the compressive strength of mortar further suggested that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic processing could more effectively exfoliate MK and endow GMK favorable dispersity, which contributed to exerting its pozzolanic, nucleation and filling effects on cement, thus improving the compressive strength of mortar. Furthermore, as for GMK group, the increasing magnitude of 1-day compressive strength was the highest (46.67%), followed by 28-day compressive strength (31.03%); While 3- and 7-day compressive strength shared the lower increasing magnitudes (19.49% and 14.05%, respectively). This finding testified that the well-dispersed GMK could accelerate early cement hydration, resulting in a significant increasing of 1-day compressive strength. This was possibly assigned to the nucleation effect of GMK. Besides, the distinct increase of 28-day compressive strength could be ascribed to favorable later cement hydration and refinement structures of GMK group. The former was attributed to the secondary hydration reaction induced by the pozzolanic effect of GMK, and the latter was probably caused by filling effect of GMK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of different paste specimens
ce:section.ce:para: To deeply analyze the reason of difference influence of UMK and GMK on the compressive strength of mortar, hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of different paste specimens were exhaustively tested.

The cumulative heat release and heat flow evolution of different cement pastes (C for blank group; UMK for 1.0 wt.% UMK-modified cement paste; GMK for 1.0 wt.% GMK-modified cement paste) were displayed in Fig. 11 . The cumulative heat releases of UMK and GMK were higher than that of blank group (Fig. 11a), indicating that the addition of UMK or GMK could promote the early hydration of cement paste to some extent. This was ascribed to the fact that with the internal pH value of cement paste increasing, the Si−O and Al−O bonds in MK tended to be broken, accelerating the dissolution and release of Al3+ and Si4+ ions. Subsequently, Si3+ and Si4+ ions underwent pozzolanic reaction with Ca(OH)2 to generate the main hydration product of C−(A)−S−H, accompanied by heat release [45,46]. It must be pointed out that due to the better dispersity of GMK, its promotion effect was stronger than that of UMK, leading to more cumulative heat release. Regarding the heat flow evolution, GMK held the largest exothermic peak value. At the same time, the related peak value came into being the slightly earliest (Fig. 11b). This also substantiated that GMK could enlarge the reaction speed of early cement hydration, conducive to refining pore structures and eventually promoting the early mechanical properties of cement-based materials.

The specific phase composition information of different pastes was recorded by XRD and TGA/DTG. In general, hydration products of MK-modified cement composites principally include C−(A)−S−H, Ca(OH)2 and ettringite/monosulfate (AFt/AFm). Meanwhile, calcium carbonate (CaCO3) is inevitably formed, ascribed to the reaction between CaCO3 and CO2 during the preparation of testing samples. As shown in Fig. 12 , all the XRD patterns of diverse pastes presented similar peaks, assigned to the hydration products such as Ca(OH)2 (peaks at 18.1°, 34° and 47.1°), unhydrated products containing dicalcium silicate (C2S, peaks at 32° and 41.2°) and tricalcium silicate (C3S, peak at 32.6°) as well as the carbonization product including CaCO3 (peak at 29.4°). There was no new diffraction peak found in MK-modified cement pastes, evidencing that the introduction of MK could not generate new hydration products. But the peak intensity, particularly for Ca(OH)2, went through an obvious change. Normally, the intensity of the main diffraction peak at 18.1° from Ca(OH)2 is proportional to its content, thus used to evaluate the degree of the hydration degree [47,48]. Namely, the higher the peak intensity, the higher the hydration degree. Compared with that of blank group, the peak intensity of 18.1° in UMK group had no marked change, while as for GMK group, the peak visibly decreased. The reason was that the pozzolanic reaction occurred in GMK group but not in UMK group, which brought about secondary hydration between Al2O3/SiO2 in MK and Ca(OH)2 in cement paste, thereby consuming a part of Ca(OH)2, and lowering its main peak intensity in GMK. Hence, Under the dual disadvantages of agglomeration and small addition, UMK failed to significantly exert pozzolanic reaction, leading to negligible enhancement in mortar compressive strength; Whereas the well dispersed GMK could efficiently provide pozzolanic reaction to produce more C−(A)−S−H, eventually promoting the 28-day compressive strength of cement composites. It should be mentioned that by virtue of its amorphous form feature, C−(A)−S−H was difficult to be detected by XRD.

Since XRD was not sensitive to minor or amorphous phases, TGA/DTG was carried out to acquire more phase composition information of various 28-day curing cement pastes (Fig. 13 ). As illustrated in TGA curves (Fig. 13a), all the pastes presented curves with the similar downward trend, and GMK group possessed the maximum mass loss, meaning that more hydration products were generated in GMK group. DTG curves (Fig. 13b) could distinguish different phases based on mass losses at different temperatures. And the more intense the mass loss hump, the greater the content of the corresponding phase. All the DTG curves showed identical mass loss peaks but with various peak intensities, indicating that the addition of UMK or GMK could not form new phases but only alter their contents, corresponding to the XRD result. More specifically, mass loss peaks at around 100 °C, 200 °C and 350 °C, 400–580 °C and 600–800 °C were attributed to the decomposition of C−(A)−S−H/AFt, AFm, Ca(OH)2 and CaCO3, respectively. With respect to blank group, GMK group held remarkably more C−(A)−S−H/AFt, while UMK group demonstrated a marginal increase of C−(A)−S−H/AFt. This inferred that owing to its better dispersion, GMK were able to promote the more formation of hydration products such as C−(A)−S−H, which conduced to boosting the mortar compressive strength. Nevertheless, UMK with a serious agglomeration state play little positive effect on the generation of hydration products under the small incorporation into cement. To better understand the effect of GMK, the content of Ca(OH)2 in different groups was calculated according to Equations. (2)–(4).

was the weight loss from DTG data curves for the dehydration process of C−(A)−S−H/AFt (105–400 °C). 0.41 indicated the conversion ratio of chemically bound water when converting CaCO3 to Ca(OH)2, and 0.24 was the ratio of chemically bound water when cement completely hydrated. Table 6 listed the calculations of W CH and α . Not surprisingly, GMK group had a lowest W CH value of 20.86%, followed by UMK group (23.74%), decreasing by 14.0% and 2.1% than that of blank group (24.25%). The reduction of Ca(OH)2 in MK-modified groups resulted from the secondary hydration between Al2O3/SiO2 in MK and Ca(OH)2 in cement paste. Remarkably, GMK group went through more secondary hydration reaction than UMK group, manifesting that high-dispersity GMK could fully exert pozzolanic effect, more propitious to improve the mechanical property of cement-based materials. This finding well coincided with the result drawn from XRD. In addition, it has been corroborated that there is a strong linear correlation between α and compressive strength of cement paste, and the compressive strength is promoted with α increasing [2,16]. Obviously, GMK group possessed the largest α value, while UMK group shared the similar α value with blank group. This deduced that well dispersed GMK with only 1.0 wt.% incorporation could boost the α value, thereby increasing the compressive strength of mortar; But due to agglomeration, small amount of UMK failed to play a significant role in the enhancement of α value.

SEM, BJH and MIP were adopted to disclose the influence of UMK and GMK on the microstructure of cement pastes. Fig. 14 depicted SEM images of different cement pastes after 28-day curing age. As for blank group (Fig. 14a), hydration products were haphazardly distributed, including a small amount of fine needle-like AFt and multitudes of granular C−(A)−S−H gels. This made its internal structure greatly loose and porous, thus resulting in the unfavorable mechanical properties. It must be stressed that on account of the cover of C−(A)−S−H gels or the limit of the selected area, the flaky Ca(OH)2 was not found. Regarding UMK group (Fig. 14b), hydration products were also not arranged uniformly. The fine needle-like AFt clearly reduced. While flaky Ca(OH)2 crystals few granular C−(A)−S−H gels covering them could be found, demonstrating that UMK failed to fully exert its pozzolanic effect due to aggregation. Meanwhile, the internal structure of UMK-containing paste was still loose and porous, thereby holding unfavorable compressive strength. These observations could deduce that aggregated UMK could not adequately exert its nucleation and filling effects, causing the hydration products haphazard distribution and making the internal structure porous. With respect to GMK group (Fig. 14c), hydration products principally containing a large number of granular C−(A)−S−H gels were evenly distributed, where C−(A)−S−H gels were more compact and covered all the large pores, giving rise to a denser internal structure. This testified that highly dispersed GMK was able to fully exert its nucleation and filling effects to effectively refine the microstructure of GMK-modified cement composites, enormously conduced to promoting their compressive strength. At the same time, there were no Ca(OH)2 crystals found in GMK group, which indicated that secondary hydration reaction caused by the pozzolanic effect of GMK consumed a large quantity of Ca(OH)2 crystals to generate more C−(A)−S−H gels, in agreement with findings of XRD and TGA/DTG. In brief, the high-dispersity GMK prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could effectively exert nucleation, filling and pozzolanic effects to significantly refine the microstructure of the hardened cement-based materials; Whereas the aggregated UMK obtained by pure ultrasonic processing failed to accomplish the above effects.

The BJH method was used to measure the pore size distributions of diverse cement pastes after curing 28 d. As displayed in Fig. 15 , GMK group owned the smallest central peak value of 15.79 nm, reducing by 25.17% and 4.01% than blank group (21.10 nm) and UMK group (16.45 nm) respectively. This suggested that through nucleation and filling effects, highly dispersed GMK in the cement matrix accelerated cement hydration and densified pore-size structure, precisely corresponding to the conclusions of SEM. In addition, GMK-modified cement paste had the most gel pores between 2 and 10 nm, which verified the more formation of C−(A)−S−H gels, also contributing to the promotion of compressive strength of mortars.

Since BJH method was difficult to detect the pore size larger than 60 nm, MIP testing was performed to characterize the influence of UMK and GMK on the structure of larger-sized pores. As shown in Fig. 16 a and Table 7 , GMK group revealed the smallest total porosity of 23.6%, decreasing by 20.8% and 9.9% than blank group (29.8%) and UMK group (26.2%) respectively. This inferred that GMK endowed the cement paste with the most refined pore structure, and the less porosity was beneficial to boost the mortar compressive strength. Furthermore, the average pore diameter and median pore diameter values were also smallest in GMK group, tremendously reducing by 10.7% and 41.2% than those of blank group respectively; On the contrary, UMK group only achieved an insignificant decrease of 2.8% and 18.3% respectively compared with blank group. This was ascribed the fact that GMK possessed better dispersity than UMK; And under the small incorporation of 1.0 wt.%, GMK could more effectively exert its nucleation, filling and pozzolanic effects to refine the pore structure and improve the compactness of cement composites, thereby ultimately promoting the mortar compressive strength. These findings were coincidental with the conclusions of SEM and BJH method. Additionally, as presented in Fig. 16b, the most frequent pore diameter had the smallest diameter value (183.17 nm) in the GMK group, significantly reducing by 78.0% and 57.8% than those of blank group (833.57 nm) and UMK group (434.10 nm) respectively. Generally speaking, the smaller the most frequent pore diameter, the greater the pore structure refinement. This supported the most refined pore structure of the GMK group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of increasing mortar compressive strength by GMK
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the analyses of hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure into account, the mechanism of increasing mortar compressive strength by GMK was properly proposed (Fig. 17 ). In detail, firstly, compared with UMK, GMK held the smaller size and larger specific surface area. As is well known, small-sized materials with larger specific surface area can provide more nucleation sites [49,50] to nucleate, precipitate and grow hydration products (i.e., nucleation effect), thereby greatly facilitating cement hydration and improving the microscopic and macroscopic structures of cement composites [51]. Thus, GMK could better offer nucleation effect to form more C−(A)−S−H, resulting in more refine microstructure for cement composites. This was supported by SEM, BJH and MIP. Secondly, GMK possessed the better dispersity than UMK, proved by sedimentation testing. The highly dispersed GMK was able to exert pozzolanic effect, making Ca(OH)2 react to the hydration product of C−(A)−S−H, thereby densifying mortar microstructure; Whereas the dissolution and release of Al3+ and Si4+ ions by agglomerated UMK were limited, thus failing to underwent the full pozzolanic reaction with Ca(OH)2. This was evidenced by hydration heat evolution and XRD. Thirdly, unquestionably, high-dispersity GMK could better achieve filling effect than aggregated UMK, giving rise to the further refined microstructure. To sum up, with reference to UMK, highly dispersed and small GMK fabricated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could fully exert nucleation, filling and pozzolanic effects to remarkably accelerate hydration process, boost the formation of C−(A)−S−H and refine the microstructure, thus enhancing the compressive strength of cement composites. It must be pointed out that in this work, GMK with only 1.0 wt.% addition enormously increased the 28-day compressive strength by 31.03%. If more GMK was added, the compressive strength of GMK-reinforced mortar could be further increased.

In summary, for the first time, this present study developed a facile GQDs-assisted ultrasonic route to effectively exfoliate and disperse MK, and detailedly investigated the effect of as-prepared GMK with a small incorporation of 1.0 wt.% on compressive strength of cement composites. The primary results were drawn as follows:
(1)
GQDs-assisted ultrasonic processing could more effectively exfoliate and disperse MK than pure ultrasonic treatment, thereby endowing GMK with better dispersity. In detail, TEM, sedimentation and nitrogen adsorption testing as well as XRD unveiled that GMK possessed smaller size (less than 3 μm) and larger SBET (6.134 m2 g−1) than MK and UMK, but shared the same crystal structure with them. Hence, GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment could efficiently delaminate MK and decrease its size, but retain its crystal structure.
(2)
According to characteristics of MK, UMK and GMK, the exfoliation and dispersion mechanism of MK caused by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was rationally ascribed to intercalation, exfoliation and surfactant effects of GQDs. The intercalation and exfoliation effects were conducive to delaminating the layer from MK and reduce its size; While the surfactant effect was considered to resist the re-aggregation of GMK.
(3)
Compared with MK, UMK with an incorporation of 1.0 wt.% had little influence on the compressive strength of mortar at any curing age; While GMK demonstrated a significant enhancement of 46.67%, 19.49%, 14.05% and 31.03% for the compressive strength of mortar at 1-, 3-, 7- and 28-day curing age respectively under the identical conditions.
(4)
On basis of the comprehensive analyses of hydration heat, phase composition and microstructure of different cement pastes, the positive effect of highly dispersed GMK on compressive strength of cement composites was that GMK only with a dosage of 1.0 wt.% were able to accelerate hydration process, boost the formation of C−(A)−S−H and refine the microstructure.

This work not only demonstrated a facile, cost-effective, scalable and eco-friendly approach for efficiently preparing high-dispersity MK, but also revealed its remarkable improvement in compressive strength of cement composites. These observations would be conducive to improving the benefits and lowering the cost of MK-like materials, allowing them to be more effectively employed in cement composites.
